chapter_title: Inorganic chemicals; organic or inorganic compounds of precious metals, of rare-earth metals, of radioactive elements or of isotopes
inclusions: Sub-Chapter I — Chemical Elements: Halogens (F, Cl, Br, I); sublimed/precipitated/colloidal sulphur; carbon blacks and other carbon forms NESOI; hydrogen, rare gases, N2, O2, B, Te, Si, P, As, Se; alkali/alkaline-earth metals; rare-earth metals, Sc, Y; mercury

Sub-Chapter II — Inorganic Acids and Inorganic Oxygen Compounds of Non-metals: HCl, chlorosulphuric acid; H2SO4, oleum; HNO3, sulphonitric acids; P2O5, H3PO4, polyphosphoric acids; boron oxides, boric acids; other inorganic acids (HF, HCN, arsenic acid, sulphamic acid) and other inorganic oxygen compounds of non-metals (CO2, SiO2, As2O5, SO2)

Sub-Chapter III — Halogen or Sulphur Compounds of Non-metals: Halides and halide oxides of non-metals (phosgene, P-chlorides, S-chlorides, thionyl chloride); sulphides of non-metals, carbon disulphide, commercial phosphorus trisulphide

Sub-Chapter IV — Inorganic Bases and Metal Oxides/Hydroxides/Peroxides: Ammonia (anhydrous or aqueous); NaOH, KOH, Na/K peroxides; Mg hydroxide/peroxide; Sr/Ba oxides/hydroxides/peroxides; ZnO, ZnO2; artificial corundum, Al2O3, Al(OH)3; Cr/Mn/Fe/Co/Ti/Pb/Zn and other metal oxides and hydroxides; hydrazine, hydroxylamine and their inorganic salts

Sub-Chapter V — Salts and Peroxysalts: Fluorides, fluorosilicates, fluoroaluminates, synthetic cryolite; chlorides (ammonium, calcium, other metal chlorides), bromides, iodides; hypochlorites, chlorites; chlorates, perchlorates, bromates, iodates; sulphides, polysulphides; dithionites, sulphoxylates; sulphites, thiosulphates; sulphates, alums, persulphates; nitrites, nitrates; phosphinates, phosphonates, phosphates, polyphosphates; carbonates, percarbonates; cyanides, complex cyanides; silicates; borates, perborates; oxometallic/peroxometallic salts (chromates, permanganates, molybdates, tungstates); other complex/double salts (aluminosilicates, fulminates, cyanates, thiocyanates)

Sub-Chapter VI — Miscellaneous: Colloidal precious metals; inorganic or organic compounds of precious metals (AgNO3, Au, Pt compounds), amalgams of precious metals; radioactive chemical elements and isotopes (natural U, enriched U-235, depleted U-235, Pu, Th, tritium, polonium, and other radioactive elements), spent nuclear fuel cartridges, radioactive residues; stable isotopes other than 28.44 (heavy water D2O, boron-10, lithium-6, helium-3); rare-earth metal compounds (La, Ce, Pr, Nd, Y, Sc); H2O2; carbides (CaC2, SiC, other); hydrides, nitrides, azides, silicides, borides; inorganic/organic compounds of mercury (excl. amalgams); phosphides; other inorganic compounds (distilled/conductivity water, liquid air, compressed air); amalgams of non-precious metals

exclusions.condition: Sodium chloride (NaCl) or magnesium oxide (MgO) whether or not pure, or other products of Section V (Chapters 25-27)
exclusions.action: Redirect to Section V — NaCl → heading 25.01; MgO → heading 25.19

exclusions.condition: Organo-inorganic compounds other than those specifically listed in Note 2 of Chapter 28
exclusions.action: Redirect to Chapter 29 (organic chemicals) or relevant chapter. Note 2 exceptions that DO stay in Ch.28 include: CO/CO2/HCN/complex cyanogen acids (28.11), halide oxides of carbon (28.12), CS2 (28.13), thiocarbonates/selenocarbonates/complex cyanates of inorganic bases (28.42), H2O2 solidified with urea (28.47), cyanogen/cyanogen halides/cyanamide (28.53)

exclusions.condition: Fertilizer products mentioned in Notes 2, 3, 4 or 5 to Chapter 31
exclusions.action: Redirect to Chapter 31 — e.g., calcium cyanamide (whether or not pure) → Chapter 31

exclusions.condition: Inorganic products of a kind used as luminophores
Glass frit and other glass in the form of powder, granules or flakes
Cultured crystals (other than optical elements) weighing not less than 2.5g each, of the halides of the alkali or alkaline-earth metals
exclusions.action: Redirect to Heading 38.24. Optical elements of alkali/alkaline-earth halides → Heading 90.01

exclusions.condition: Precious or semi-precious stones (natural, synthetic or reconstructed) or dust/powder of such stones
exclusions.action: Redirect to Headings 71.02 to 71.05; precious metals or precious metal alloys → Chapter 71

exclusions.condition: Metals (pure or not), metal alloys or cermets, including sintered metal carbides (metal carbides sintered with a metal)
exclusions.action: Redirect to Section XV (base metals). Note: pure metal carbides NOT sintered with metal → 28.49. Metal carbides sintered with metal (e.g. WC-Co) → Section XV

classification_rules.rule: Note 1 — Chapter 28 applies ONLY to chemically defined compounds (with specific permitted additives)
classification_rules.description: Headings of Ch.28 apply only to: (a) separate chemical elements and separate CHEMICALLY DEFINED compounds, whether or not containing impurities; (b) the above dissolved in water; (c) the above dissolved in other solvents only if dissolution is a normal/necessary packaging method for safety or transport and the solvent does not render the product suitable for specific use; (d) the above with added stabiliser (including anti-caking agent) necessary for preservation/transport; (e) the above with added anti-dusting agent or colouring substance for identification or safety — provided additions do not create specific-use suitability. If a product is a MIXTURE of inorganic chemicals for a specific use, it typically goes to Ch.38 or the relevant application chapter.

classification_rules.rule: Note 2 — Only specific carbon compounds belong in Ch.28; all other carbon compounds → Ch.29
classification_rules.description: The great majority of carbon compounds belong to Chapter 29 (organic). Chapter 28 accepts ONLY these carbon compounds: (1) Oxides of carbon (CO, CO2), HCN, and other simple/complex cyanogen acids → 28.11; (2) Halide oxides of carbon (phosgene, etc.) → 28.12; (3) Carbon disulphide → 28.13; (4) Thiocarbonates, selenocarbonates, tellurocarbonates, complex cyanates of inorganic bases → 28.42; (5) H2O2 solidified with urea → 28.47; (6) Carbon oxysulphide, thiocarbonyl halides, cyanogen, cyanogen halides, cyanamide and its metal derivatives → 28.53. All other carbon-containing compounds → Chapter 29 or other chapters.

classification_rules.rule: Note 5 — Headings 28.26 to 28.42 apply ONLY to metal or ammonium salts/peroxysalts; double/complex salts → 28.42
classification_rules.description: Headings 28.26–28.42 exclusively cover METAL salts or AMMONIUM salts or PEROXYSALTS. Any double or complex salts → heading 28.42 unless the context otherwise requires. This means if a salt has two different metal cations, or is a complex salt with coordination bonds → 28.42.

classification_rules.rule: Note 6 — Heading 28.44 strict scope for radioactive elements
classification_rules.description: Heading 28.44 covers: (a) Tc(43), Pm(61), Po(84), and ALL elements with atomic number >84 (astatine, radon, francium, radium, actinium, thorium, Pa, U, and transuranic elements); (b) Natural or artificial radioactive isotopes (including of precious/base metals); (c) Their compounds (inorganic or organic, whether or not chemically defined); (d) Alloys/dispersions/cermets/ceramics/mixtures containing these elements/isotopes/compounds with specific radioactivity >74 Bq/g; (e) Spent fuel cartridges; (f) Radioactive residues. Note: stable U-238 in natural uranium still → 28.44 because natural U contains radioactive isotopes.

classification_rules.rule: Note 8 — Doped semiconductor elements: form determines Ch.28 vs. 38.18
classification_rules.description: Chemical elements (e.g., silicon, selenium) doped for use in electronics: IF in unworked forms as drawn, or in form of cylinders or rods → Chapter 28. IF cut in the form of DISCS, WAFERS or similar forms → heading 38.18. The cutting into disc/wafer shape is the trigger for moving from Ch.28 to 38.18.

The form of sulphur determines the chapter: natural sulphur (raw, not processed beyond basic physical treatment) → heading 25.03 (Chapter 25). Sulphur that has been sublimed, precipitated, or is in colloidal form (chemically processed) → heading 28.02 (Chapter 28). This is a key Ch.25 vs. Ch.28 boundary for sulphur.

Heading 28.04 sub-classifies silicon (non-doped form) by silicon content: ≥99.99% Si → subheading 2804.61 (ultra-pure, semiconductor-grade silicon, but only if not cut to discs/wafers — if cut to discs/wafers → 38.18). <99.99% Si → subheading 2804.69 (metallurgical-grade silicon, ferrosilicon-like purity).

classification_rules.rule: Earth colours with ≥70% Fe₂O₃ → 28.21; below threshold → Chapter 25
classification_rules.description: Earth colours containing 70% or more by weight of combined iron evaluated as Fe2O3 → heading 28.21 (iron oxides and hydroxides; earth colours). Earth colours with less than 70% Fe₂O₃ → Chapter 25 (they remain as mineral earth pigments in crude state). Heading 28.21 also covers chemically pure iron oxides (Fe2O3, Fe3O4, FeO, etc.) and iron hydroxides.

classification_rules.rule: Carbides: chemically defined carbides → 28.49; carbides sintered with metal (cermet) → Section XV
classification_rules.description: Heading 28.49 covers CARBIDES whether or not chemically defined: calcium carbide (CaC2) → 2849.10; silicon carbide (SiC) → 2849.20; other carbides → 2849.90. EXCEPTION: Metal carbides SINTERED with a metal (e.g., cemented tungsten carbide, WC-Co 'hardmetal') → Section XV (treated as cermets/metal-ceramic composites, typically heading 82.09 or 84 series inserts).
